data.title: How to assess the September jobs report in the absence of BLS data
data.description:
If the Bureau of Labor Statistics were issuing its September Employment Situation report on Friday, economists were expecting a headline payrolls number rebounding some from August, but significantly weaker than a year ago. The unemployment rate is expected to stay at 4.3%.

Allison Shrivastava, economist at Indeed Hiring Lab, describes the current labor market as "stagnant." The U.S. non-farms payroll number's three-month moving average has steadily declined, she said. In August, the three-month average of the number of jobs added to the U.S. economy was 29K, down from 55K in June and 82K in August 2024.

CNN reported on Thursday that the BLS has collected its September labor data and is likely ready for release. That makes sense as the shutdown came just a few days before the report was due to be released. Data is generally collected the calendar week that contains the 12th of the month.

"The employment data is incredibly important, especially when we're in an economy that is very much 'low fire, low hire,' and we're really looking to any kind of indication of the direction that we're headed in," Indeed's Shrivastava said. Still, there are some private sources of data that can supplement the government data. For example, Indeed has job postings data.

She's looking at sector-level job postings to give an indication of how health care is doing vs. all other sectors. In recent months, health care has been a main engine of jobs creation. More than half of the jobs have been in health care and social services sectors.

Looking at the three-month moving average of jobs added to the economy, "we're still adding jobs to the economy, but much less so than we were," she said.

The headline numbers for job postings are still above prepandemic levels, however, all but one sector have declined year-over-year, Shrivastava said. "We had a really strong economy in 2022," she noted. "But it's residual strength that's bolstering our economy now, rather than sustainable strength."

If the jobs report were distributed on Friday, she'd be looking at the diffusion, or the distribution of jobs across industries. For example, will the September number continue to show that most of the jobs growth is in health care? One factor that could reduce jobs growth in health care is reduced immigration and the new $100K fee for H1-B visas, as that sector is heavily reliant on non-domestic workers, Indeed's Shrivastava said.

She'd also be looking at construction, which has been faltering recently. In August, the construction industry lost 10K jobs in June through August, according to the August jobs report.

"One of the most important metrics in today's labor market to watch — and I don't think it's really talked about enough — is churn — the layoffs rate and the quits rate, she said.

The economy may be adding some jobs, and the unemployment rate is pretty low, "but that's not all you need for a healthy economy," she said. "You need churn. You need movement."

Another sign of trouble is that the duration of unemployment is increasing, she said.

As for the state of the labor market, "we've been frozen, and now we're kind of past the point of frozen, and now we're going to have some deterioration because of that freezing," Shrivastava said.

What we know about the labor market: 

  • Chicago Fed real-time unemployment forecast: September 2025 4.34% vs. 4.32% in August and 4.09% in September 2024.
    • Hiring rate 45.22% in September vs. 45.61% in August and 46.96% a year ago.
    • Layoffs and other separations rate: 2.10% vs. 2.09% in August and 2.06% in September 2024.
  • ADP private sector employment data: -32K in September vs. -3K in August.
  • August JOLTS: Job openings 7.227M vs. 7.208M in July and 7.649M in August 2024; Hiring 5.126M vs. 5.240M in July and 5.230M in August 2024.
  • Job Cuts: Through September 2025, 946.4K vs. 609.2K in the first nine months of 2024, according to Challenger, Gray & Christmas.
  • Jobless claims: Initial claims 4-week moving average 237.5K as of Sept. 20, vs. 225.3K a year ago; continuing claims, 4-week moving average: 1.93M as of Sept. 13 vs. 1.84M a year ago.
  • Number of unemployed persons per job opening: in August 2025, 1.0 vs. 0.5 in September 2022, according to the BLS.
  • San Francisco Fed weekly labor market stress indicator at 7 as of Sept. 13, 2025, slipping from 10 at July 19, and still far lower than 51 through most of 2020.
  • Challenge, Gray & Christmas projects that hiring for the holiday retail season may be under 500,000 in the last three months of the year, the smallest seasonal gain in 16 years.

"There's a lot of nuances below the headline number in our economy right now, which is why paying attention to a lot of indicators is more important than ever," Indeed's Shrivastava said.

data.title: TD SYNNEX announces CFO transition
data.description:
• TD SYNNEX (NYSE:SNX) on Thursday announced the appointment of David Jordan as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer [CFO], effective immediately.
  • Jordan, previously Senior Vice President and Americas CFO, succeeds Marshall Witt, who is departing the company for a new role outside the organization.
  • Jordan, who joined TD SYNNEX in 2014, brings extensive experience in finance, mergers and acquisitions, and investor relations.
  • SNX is -0.36% to $163.79.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Q3 tech sector gainers & losers: Western Digital tops the chart, with Gartner, Fiserv among worst performers
data.description:
The third quarter was marked by renewed uncertainty regarding inflation concerns as investors grappled with shifting expectations around Federal Reserve policy. 

Investor sentiment was further shaken by continued geopolitical tensions, including worries around global supply chains and rising energy prices. Still, by the end of the quarter, markets regained some footing, buoyed by cooling inflation data and optimism that the Fed might be nearing the end of its tightening cycle. 

As Q3 draws to a close, the S&P 500 (SP500) has already rallied 13.2% year-to-date. Looking ahead to Q4, investors will be watching whether the strong outperformers can continue carrying the market or if renewed weakness among the bottom decile will reassert pressure. 

With the U.S. government now in shutdown, markets face an additional layer of uncertainty just as the next leg of the cycle begins.

Let’s take a closer look at how the information technology sector performed over the past three months. The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK), which tracks the S&P 500’s information technology sector, rose 12% in the third quarter, compared to an about 8% rise in the benchmark S&P 500 Index. 

Industries Q3 Performance

The Software & Services (SP500-4510) sub-sector rose more than 3% in the last quarter, while the Technology Hardware & Equipment (SP500-4520) sub-sector saw a gain of more than 21%. Meanwhile, the Semiconductor & Semiconductor Equipment (SP500-4530) industry rose more than 20% in the July-September quarter. 

U.S. stock fund flow in and out of the sector was mostly stable throughout the quarter, with an increased inflow and outflow in the month of September. The Information Technology-focused ETF had a net inflow of $1.20B. 

Top 5 movers of Q3: 

Gainers: 

Western Digital Corp (WDC) +98.53%

Seagate Technology (STX) +69.04% 

Intel Corp (INTC) +64.26%

Corning (GLW) +57.66%

Teradyne (TER) +49.84%

Losers: 

Gartner (IT) -36.53%

Fiserv (FI) -26.88%

Godaddy (GDDY) -24.65%

Accenture (ACN) -19.38%

Jack Henry & Associates (JKHY) -18.47% 

What Quantitative Measures say?

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating has given XLK a BUY rating, with a score of 4.31 out of 5. The rating system has given the technology sector tracking fund an A+ in expenses and liquidity and a C on risk. 

What do analysts expect?

The Information Technology sector has recorded the largest percentage increase in estimated earnings of all eleven sectors since the start of the quarter at 4.3%. 

According to a recent FactSet analysis, the Information Technology sector is expected to report the highest year-over-year earnings growth rate of all eleven sectors at 20.9%. 

At the industry level, all 6 industries in the sector are projected to report year-over-year earnings growth, with the Semiconductors & Semiconductor Equipment industry expected to be the largest contributor to earnings growth for the sector. 

Seeking Alpha analyst Komal Sarwar says the tech sector’s rally is driven by robust earnings growth and AI demand, not speculation or bubble dynamics. 

“Valuations remain reasonable given earnings momentum, with limited risk of a valuation-driven correction; economic growth and potential rate cuts further support the sector,” Sarwar added.

data.title: Absci spikes as J.P. Morgan starts at Buy ahead of key catalyst
data.description:
• Absci (NASDAQ:ABSI) shares traded higher on Thursday after J.P. Morgan initiated its coverage on the AI-driven biotech with a Buy recommendation ahead of a key early-stage trial readout for the company’s lead asset ABS-101 expected this year.
  • Analyst Brian Cheng and the team noted that Absci (NASDAQ:ABSI) is “at the cusp” of a key catalyst, with an interim readout scheduled for later this year.
  • With its Q2 results in August, the company said that interim data from a Phase 1 first-in-human study for ABS-101, designed to test the anti-TL1A antibody in healthy volunteers, will be disclosed before the end of this year.
  • Arguing that TL1A could be an attractive target to test Absci’s (NASDAQ:ABSI) platform, Cheng and the team opined that it could also help the company win collaborations.

data.title: Investor pushback on JDE Peet's acquisition drags Keurig Dr Pepper to 4-yr low
data.description:
Shares of Keurig Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP) fell to a four-year low on Thursday, pressured by negative sentiment around its $23B purchase of Dutch coffee and tea group JDE Peet’s.

Since announcing the deal, Keurig Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP) shares have lost 28% in value, illustrating what BNP Paribas analyst Kevin Grundy calls “one of the worst-received deals by investors in the consumer sector” that the firm has ever seen.

The  deal will position KDP as a global coffee leader, second only to Nestle, with $16B of pro forma long-term sales and $3.1B of EBITDA. KDP’s coffee portfolio will now have four $1 billion brands including Keurig, Jacobs, Peet’s, and L’OR, and 6 brands grossing over $500 million with a geographical balance of sales across North America, Europe, and 20% spread out across the remainder of the globe.

Once the acquisition is completed, KDP will split off its coffee and beverage business into two separate entities.

So why has the stock unraveled and shaved $11 billion off KDP’s valuation?

The lack of clarity regarding the complex mechanics of the split coupled with a resulting increase in debt has unnerved investors and some Wall Street analysts with BNP Paribas and Barclays both downgrading the stock.

“KDP’s fundamental setup no longer offers the compelling relative clarity that we had liked in the name previously,” Barclay’s Lauren Lieberman said, while BNP Paribas’ Grundy expects “risk to current expectations, deal risk, and credibility setback all likely to weigh on KDP’s multiple.”

Seeking Alpha analyst, The Value Investor, did the math and concluded that with the increased debt necessary to pay for the deal with its current debt results in a leverage ratio of 6x EBITDA, not the 5.2x EBITDA the company states.

“In between all these moving parts—that of a massive acquisition, a separation, and some questions on the numbers – left investors confused,” The Value Investor said, pointing to drop in the share price from $35 to $27 within weeks.

Once KDP outlines the separation plan and explains the discrepancy between leverage ratios, KDP shares should find a bottom and begin regaining lost ground amid expectations that the value of two separate entities (beverages and coffee) are worth more than the sum-of-the-parts.

data.title: SA Asks: Which tech companies could be acquired near term?
data.description:
Which tech companies could be acquired in the next few months?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Noah Cox, The Techie and  Oliver Rodzianko for their picks.

Noah Cox: I think there are a ton of opportunities in the traditional fintech space that people are not thinking enough about. Companies like PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) represent huge fintech on-ramps for big tech companies to use in an attempt to take advantage of the GENIUS Act and payment processing disruption. The future of AI agents will be closely married with payment processing. Big tech companies will want to own both ends of this. 

Another company on this front is Western Union (NYSE:WU), which mainly does wire transfers. They have a huge opportunity for international stablecoin on-ramps. I expect big tech companies to take notice. 

The Techie: SentinelOne (NYSE:S) stands out to me in the near term as a compelling acquisition candidate in cybersecurity. The market is extremely cautious about the company, as profitability and scale remain works in progress. 

In 2Q FY26, total ARR grew 24%, with a 79% gross margin, and roughly half of quarterly bookings came from non-endpoint. Purple AI is growing triple digits with >30% attach, AI SIEM is now on AWS’s (AMZN) marketplace, and the new Flex licensing model already landed an 8-figure TCV win. While these are strong signals of customer demand and platform adoption, execution risks and thin margins leave SentinelOne (NYSE:S) vulnerable. 

But said vulnerability is what makes the company attractive to larger strategics, in my opinion. CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) could remove a major rival and deepen its AI-driven security portfolio (though antitrust scrutiny may complicate this path). On the hyperscaler side, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) could absorb SentinelOne to expand its fast-growing security arms and differentiate its cloud offerings with stronger autonomous endpoint and AI SIEM capabilities.

Oliver Rodzianko: Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) is one of the clearest takeout setups right now, operating in quantum. Quanta Computer (OTC:QUCCF) already bought a $35 million stake, locked shares for three years, and signed a five-year, $250 million-each collaboration to manufacture Rigetti’s non-QPU hardware. This kind of industrial partnership commonly evolves into full ownership once the supply chain and IP integration are proven. 

However, this isn’t exactly a distressed play, as Rigetti’s $570 million in cash with no debt on the balance sheet gives it operating security. Instead, Quanta will be looking to secure IP and talent rather than obtain pure value-based upside. If Rigetti (NASDAQ:RGTI) can hit execution triggers, like its first SOW by the end of 2025, with Quanta raising its stake and new system orders tied to Quanta hardware, then Quanta (OTC:QUCCF) is the natural acquirer.

data.title: Government shutdown has limited market impact, but labor fragility adds risk – PNC’s Yung-Yu Ma
data.description:
PNC Asset Management chief investment strategist Yung-Yu Ma believes the current government shutdown will likely have a muted effect on markets, despite entering its second day.

In an interview with CNBC, Ma noted that while markets typically shrug off shutdowns, the current economic environment presents unique considerations. The S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP:IND) pulled back on Wednesday after hitting fresh record highs at the market open.

“We still don’t expect it to have a major impact, but it is a different time when there’s a bit more fragility in the labor market,” Ma said.

He emphasized that unlike previous shutdowns, today’s political and economic climate introduces additional uncertainty, particularly regarding the labor market. The strategist also referenced potential permanent cuts to the federal workforce, which could extend the impact of the shutdown beyond temporary effects seen historically.

Despite these concerns, Ma maintains an optimistic outlook for the broader economy. “We still believe the economy is on a positive trajectory here,” he said, explaining that “both the economy and the markets can do well with a weakening labor market.”

This assessment stems from robust business spending and strong consumption among upper-income households, which continue to drive economic growth regardless of labor market conditions.

The strategist highlighted that technology (XLK) and AI (ARTY), (AIEQ) investments are providing significant economic momentum with “a long runway” ahead.

Also, while labor market softening affects many companies and individuals, Ma noted that economic strength isn’t solely dependent on employment figures. The weakening seen in recent indicators like the ADP report reflects sectoral impacts rather than systemic economic vulnerability.

“The trajectory overall is favorable for the markets and the economy,” he said, though acknowledging that labor market weakening “is something that the Fed is probably going to be addressing later this month and throughout the rest of the year.”

data.title: Occidental plunges as OxyChem sale draws analyst criticism over timing, price
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) -7.5% in Thursday's trading, making it one of the largest decliners on the S&P 500, after agreeing to sell its OxyChem chemicals arm to Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7 billion, as the company tries to slash debt after years of costly acquisitions.

Occidental's (NYSE:OXY) "problem has been getting our debt down faster," CEO Vicki Hollub told CNBC, following acquisitions of Anadarko Petroleum and CrownRock in 2019 and 2024, respectively, and the company plans to use $6.5 billion in proceeds from the Berkshire deal to pay down debt, achieving its target level of principal debt below $15 billion.

This move will "unlock our stock, and allow shareholders to feel more comfortable, hopefully, to add to their positions and others to come in," Hollub said. "So now we're going to be able to start our sharing purchase program again... This is the last step that we needed in our major transformation that we started 10 years ago."

But the deal's timing is not ideal, TD Cowen analysts said, as the transaction will help the company pay down debt, yet it comes as capital expenditures tied to OxyChem's multi-year growth initiatives are peaking, meaning the free cash flow inflection expected in coming years will be lost.

Analysts at Roth MKM said the sale could weigh on free cash flow growth in coming years, as the unit was expected to contribute significantly to expansion.

Scotiabank analyst Paul Cheng noted the transaction price seemed low, as he had previously estimated the division's value at $12 billion.

data.title: Google, Amazon, and Trade Desk caught in ad tech price war for media dollars - report
data.description:
In the world of online advertising, demand-side platforms are in the midst of a price war, and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is the latest to ease costs on its ad tech, according to a Thursday report by Ad Age, citing media buyers.

The move comes as The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) faces heightened scrutiny over its fees while rivals such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) set the pace with deep discounts. The top DSPs are competing heavily for ad dollars this year by lowering fees to lure in businesses, the report said.

Google is feeling the squeeze from Amazon, which has applied its retail-style pricing power to the DSP market and has offered low fees to grow the footprint of its DSP. As a result, the internet search giant is relaxing DSP fees, especially on programmatic guaranteed deals, according to ad agency leaders, who spoke with Ad Age on condition of anonymity.

Programmatic guaranteed deals are prearranged between the buyer and publisher—often in connected TV upfronts—where the DSP functions more like an ad server, simply facilitating the order.

“We now see Google matching Amazon’s close-to-zero rates,” an agency leader told Ad Age on condition of anonymity.Programmatic guaranteed dollars make up a major share of DSP deals in the upfronts, when advertisers lock in placements across apps such as Disney+ (DIS), Peacock (CMCSA), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), Tubi (FOX) (FOXA), and others.

Ad agencies and brands are tightening budgets and scrutinizing ad tech costs. Google has taken the hint from Amazon after seeing the e-commerce and tech giant's budget-friendly deals, which fueled growth of its DSP, agency leaders told Ad Age.

That dynamic has put The Trade Desk at a disadvantage, since it doesn’t share the same business model and relies more on fees, the report said.

The Trade Desk has been in a tough position relative to Google and Amazon, partly because it doesn’t have a sprawling media ecosystem like they do. Also, ad tech is in a moment that is challenging for DSPs, as the supply side—which traditionally worked only with the publishers that sell ad inventory—now directly approaches agencies and brands, the report said.

Supply-side platforms such as Magnite (MGNI), PubMatic (PUBM), and Index Exchange are developing services that curate inventory for advertisers. In that dynamic, where the advertiser arranges curated deals directly through SSPs, the data and logic from DSPs are valued less, agency leaders told Ad Age.The Trade Desk wants to avoid programmatic ‘race to the bottom,’ the report said. The company is responding to the fee wars by pointing advertisers to the success of campaigns run through the platform.

“Rather than create a race to the bottom, our focus is on how can we deliver as much value as possible,” Samantha Jacobson, The Trade Desk’s chief strategy officer, told Ad Age.

The Trade Desk is competing on robust data and identity products that target ads more efficiently, and yes, there are fees associated with that, Jacobson said.

Earlier this week, The Trade Desk introduced another product that it hopes will increase the value of its DSP. It launched an upgrade to its third-party data marketplace, which has connections to Walmart (WMT), Kroger (KR), Walgreens, Dollar General (DG), and other sources.

data.title: Consumer Discretionary sector jumps 9.8% in Q3; Tesla takes lead, Carmax bottoms
data.description:
The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLY), which holds a 10.65% weightage on the S&P 500, surged 9.8% in Q3, while the broader index S&P 500 ETF (SPY) rose 7.86% in Q3.

Retailing (SP500-2550), which holds highest weightage to the index, ended the quarter with 3.28% gain, Consumer Services (SP500-2530) and Consumer Durables and Apparel (SP500-2520) fell -3.22% whereas, Automobiles & Components (SP500-2510) surged 43.85% higher in the third quarter.

Top 5 consumer discretionary movers of Q3:

Gainers:

Tesla (TSLA) +40.99%

D.R. Horton (DHI) +29.63%

Tapestry (TPR) +27.22%

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) +24.69%

Expedia Group (EXPE) +23.26%

Losers:

Carmax (KMX) -35.19%

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) -29.90%

Lululemon Athletica (LULU) -27.74%

LKQ (LKQ) -19.45%

Amcor (AMCR) -14.69%

Fund Flows

The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF recorded a Q3 outflow of -$155.33M, compared to an inflow of $709.67M in the second quarter.

What Quantitative Measures say

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating has given XLY a Buy rating, with a score of 3.90 out of 5. The rating system has given the consumer discretionary sector tracking fund an A+ in liquidity and expenses, A in dividends, A- in momentum, and a D in risk.

What Analysts Expect

The consumer discretionary sector has faced challenges in 2025 due to economic uncertainty and sector-specific headwinds. Seeking Alpha analyst OA Research has quoted, "Given macro risks and tech-driven market leadership, I recommend VOO over XLY for the remainder of 2025 due to lower risk and better sector exposure."

Meanwhile, Rob Isbitts says XLY remains a useful long-term tool for accessing consumer trends, adding that "despite the familiarity, it is bound to be as cyclical as the broad economy."
